stimulus: In recent decades, scholars have investigated the origins of contemporary Japanese ramen culture, and they have offered competing accounts. Some, like food historian Lila Hartmann, argue that ramen is essentially a localized adaptation of nineteenth-century Chinese noodle traditions brought by immigrant cooks. Others, including ethnographer Daichi Okamoto, contend that while Chinese influence is undeniable, modern ramen owes its character primarily to twentieth-century Japanese postwar innovations in broth, machine-made noodles, and regional experimentation.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Okamoto’s argument?

answerOptions.content: Several documented ramen shops in early twentieth-century port cities employed cooks who had trained in Chinese kitchens before immigrating to Japan.
Many ramen shops across Japan today display menus that share strikingly similar dish names and presentation styles.
The defining broths and noodle textures of most regional ramen styles can be traced to specific Japanese innovations developed between 1945 and 1970.
Ramen has become one of the most internationally recognized Japanese dishes, with restaurants in more than fifty countries.

rationale: Choice C is the best answer because it presents a finding that, if true, would directly support Okamoto’s claim that modern ramen’s defining characteristics emerged from postwar Japanese innovation. If the characteristic broths and noodle textures originated in 1945–1970 Japan, then modern ramen’s character is primarily shaped by Japanese postwar developments rather than by the imported Chinese tradition. Choice A actually supports Hartmann’s view by emphasizing Chinese origins. Choice B speaks to homogeneity, not origin. Choice D speaks to popularity, not origin.
